key_insight: A machine-learning pipeline can separate muon and hadron tracks in the GRAPES-3 muon telescope to improve reconstruction of cosmic-ray shower particles.
engineering_relevance: Better muon/hadron discrimination reduces background noise and helps estimate primary cosmic-ray properties more reliably in large detector systems.
method: Simulated proton showers with CORSIKA and Geant4 to generate detector-hit signatures for muons and hadrons.
Trained single-particle classifiers including decision trees, random forests, neural networks, and XGBoost.
Built a graph neural network with edge convolutions for multiparticle node-level classification, then used a Dynamic Reduction Network for particle-count regression.
evidence_signal: On single-particle classification, XGBoost achieved the best reported accuracy at 88.7% on the simulated dataset.
watch_item: Results are based on simulated showers, so performance may change when the pipeline is applied to real detector data and overlapping events.
paper_title: Machine learning pipeline for identifying tracks of muons and hadrons at GRAPES-3 muon telescope
